snp4.16:1.1:
Ven. Sāriputta:
“Never before have I seen or heard
snp4.16:1.4: of a teacher with such lovely speech
snp4.16:1.5: come, together with his following from Tusita heaven,
snp4.16:2.1: as the One with Eyes who appears to the world with its devas,
snp4.16:2.4: having arrived at delight all alone.
snp4.16:3.1: To that One Awakened— unentangled, Such, un-
snp4.16:3.4: I have come desiring a question on behalf of the many here who are fettered:
snp4.16:4.3: the root of a tree, a cemetery,
snp4.16:5.2: how many are the fears there
snp4.16:6.1: how many the dangers in the world
snp4.16:6.2: for the monk going the direction he never has gone
snp4.16:7.1: What should be the ways of his speech?
snp4.16:7.2: What should be his range there of action?
snp4.16:7.3: What should be a resolute monk’s habits & practices?
snp4.16:8.4:
would he blow away his own impurities as a silver smith, those in molten silver?”
The Buddha:
snp4.16:9.1: “I will tell you as one who knows, what is comfort for one disaffected
snp4.16:9.3: if he’s resorting to a place remote,
snp4.16:9.4: desiring self-awakening in line with the Dhamma.
snp4.16:10.1: An enlightened monk, living circumscribed, mindful, shouldn’t fear the five fears:
snp4.16:11.1: shouldn’t be fazed by those following another’s teaching
snp4.16:11.2: even on seeing their manifold threats;
snp4.16:11.3: should prevail over still other further dangers
snp4.16:11.4: as he seeks what is skillful.
snp4.16:12.1: Touched by the touch of disease, hunger,
snp4.16:12.2: he should endure cold & inordinate heat.
snp4.16:12.3: He with no home, in many ways touched by these things,
snp4.16:12.4: striving, should make firm his persistence.
snp4.16:13.1: He shouldn’t commit a theft, shouldn’t speak a lie,
snp4.16:13.2: should touch with thoughts of goodwill beings firm & infirm.
snp4.16:13.3: Conscious of when his mind is stirred up & turbid,
snp4.16:13.4:
he should dispel it:
‘It’s on the side of the Dark One.’
snp4.16:14.1: He shouldn’t come under the sway of anger or pride.
snp4.16:14.2: Having dug up their root he would stand firm.
snp4.16:14.4: Then, when prevailing —yes— he’d prevail over notions of dear & undear.
snp4.16:15.1: Deferring to discernment enraptured with what’s admirable,
snp4.16:15.3: should conquer discontent in his isolated spot,
snp4.16:15.4: should conquer these four thoughts of lament:
snp4.16:16.1: ‘What will I eat, or where will I eat?
snp4.16:16.2:
How badly I slept.
Tonight where will I sleep?’
snp4.16:16.4: he should subdue— one under training, wandering without home.
snp4.16:17.1: Receiving food & cloth at appropriate times,
snp4.16:17.2: he should have a sense of enough for the sake of contentment.
snp4.16:17.3: Guarded in regard to these things going restrained into a village,
snp4.16:17.4: even when harassed he shouldn’t say a harsh word.
snp4.16:18.2: committed to jhāna, he should be continually wakeful.
snp4.16:18.4: he should cut off any penchant to conjecture or worry.
snp4.16:19.1: When reprimanded with words, he should—mindful— rejoice;
snp4.16:19.2: should smash any rigidity toward his fellows in the holy life;
snp4.16:19.3: should utter skillful words that are not untimely;
snp4.16:19.4: should give no mind to the gossip people might say.
snp4.16:20.1: And then there are in the world the five kinds of dust
snp4.16:20.2: for whose subduing, mindful, he should train:
snp4.16:20.3: With regard to forms, sounds, tastes,
snp4.16:20.4: smells, & tactile sensations he should conquer passion;
snp4.16:21.1: with regard to these things he should subdue his desire.
snp4.16:21.2: A monk, mindful, his mind well released,
snp4.16:21.3: contemplating the right Dhamma at the right times,
snp4.16:21.4: on coming to oneness should annihilate darkness,” the Blessed One said.
